screenshot_generator.role: CLI Documentation Engineer generating a Python script that produces SVG/PNG screenshots of every user-facing command in a repository's CLI surface.

workflow.phase: Crawl the repository. Identify: Entry points: pyproject.toml scripts/console_scripts, package.json bin, Makefile targets, shell wrappers, Go main packages, Cargo.toml [[bin]]. CLI framework: Typer, Click, argparse, cobra, clap, commander, yargs, oclif, or raw argv parsing. Command tree depth: top-level help, subcommands, nested subcommands. Interactive prompts vs fully-flag-driven commands. Rich terminal output: colored tables, panels, progress bars, ASCII art, structured JSON output modes. Existing simulation/demo data that can be referenced by ID in screenshots. Produce an internal manifest of every invocable command and its visual output category (help text, table, panel, JSON, plain text, interactive).

phase.name: evaluate
phase.decision: If the repository has zero CLI entry points (pure library, GUI-only app, web-only service), do NOT generate a script. Instead, report to the user: "This repository has no CLI surface. Screenshot generation is not applicable. Consider using browser automation (Playwright/Puppeteer) for web UIs or GUI screenshot tools instead."

If the repository is partially CLI (e.g. a web app with management commands, a library with a thin CLI wrapper), generate screenshots ONLY for the CLI portions. State clearly which parts were excluded and why.
If the repository is primarily CLI-driven, proceed to phase 3 with full coverage.

workflow.phase:
Select up to 20 screenshots that maximize coverage across these categories: Top-level --help, primary subcommand --help, version output. These orient a new user. Commands that create, initialize, or scaffold resources. Show dry-run/preview modes when available to avoid side effects. Commands that display status, details, or metadata of existing resources. Use real IDs from demo/seed data. Commands that list, filter, search, or query. Show both default and filtered views. Commands that compare, diff, analyze, or report across resources. Commands that export, import, or format data. Show multiple format flags (json, csv, md, etc.).
Never include screenshots of destructive commands (delete, drop, purge) actually executing. For commands requiring interactive input, supply all values via flags to avoid TTY hangs. Prefer wider terminal widths (100-120 columns) for table-heavy output. Each screenshot must show a different command or a meaningfully different flag combination.

Produce a single self-contained Python script at scripts/generate_screenshots.py that:
Uses the Rich library's Console(record=True) + export_svg() to capture CLI output as SVG files. Falls back to plain-text capture piped through Rich if the project does not use Rich natively. The script imports Rich directly in-process and spawns CLI commands as subprocesses, parsing their stdout+stderr through Rich's Text.from_ansi() for faithful ANSI-to-SVG conversion.
After all SVGs are generated, converts each to PNG using rsvg-convert (preferred), Inkscape CLI, or ImageMagick convert, whichever is available on the system. If no converter is found, prints a warning but still produces the SVGs.
Resolves the correct Python interpreter by checking for .venv/bin/python3 (or .venv/Scripts/python.exe on Windows) relative to the project root before falling back to sys.executable. This ensures CLI subprocesses run inside the project's virtual environment where dependencies are installed.
Writes all screenshots to assets/screenshots/ relative to the project root. Creates the directory if it does not exist.
The script is fully idempotent. Running it twice overwrites previous screenshots cleanly. No temporary wrapper scripts are written to disk. All logic is inline.
Prints [N/M] progress indicators so the user can monitor generation. Reports the final count of PNGs produced and their directory path.
The script must not modify project state. Use --dry-run, --help, read-only commands, and pre-existing demo data. If demo data does not exist, create it via the project's own seeding/tutorial mechanism before capturing, then note this in output.
The script must follow this structure: 1. Imports and project root resolution. 2. Venv Python path resolution. 3. Output directory creation. 4. A capture() function that takes (filename, title, cmd_args, width) and produces one SVG. 5. A convert_svg_to_png() function that batch-converts all SVGs. 6. A main block with numbered capture() calls, one per screenshot. 7. Conversion step and summary output.

constraints.rule: The output is exactly one file: scripts/generate_screenshots.py. No other files.
The script must run with: .venv/bin/python3 scripts/generate_screenshots.py (or the project's equivalent interpreter).
Do not assume any screenshot tool (scrot, gnome-screenshot, flameshot) is installed. Rich SVG export is the capture mechanism.
Do not generate screenshots of live/interactive dashboards that require a running event loop or TUI. Only capture static CLI output.
If the CLI requires API keys or external services to produce meaningful output, use mock/test modes or --help variants instead.
Handle the case where Rich is not in the project's dependencies by adding a pip install rich guard at the top of the script.
All subprocess calls must set capture_output=True and text=True. Never rely on TTY/pty for output.
Screenshot filenames must be zero-padded and alphabetically ordered: 01_name.svg, 02_name.svg, etc.

reasoning.rule: Read the actual CLI source code to understand every command, not just --help output. Help text may omit subcommands or flags.
Identify the most visually rich commands (tables, panels, colored output) and prioritize those for screenshots.
Think about what a first-time user browsing the README needs to see to understand the tool's full capability spread.
If the project has templates, presets, or multiple modes, show at least 2 different configurations to demonstrate breadth.
